explicit_text: I was living in Pittsburgh when I first tried to get into the Navy aviation program. The Navy team from Philadelphia came over once a month to examine candidates. Pittsburgh was my home before I moved to Oklahoma City after the war. I remember it as a colder place compared to Oklahoma City, which I later found much warmer and cleaner.
implicit_text: I was living in the city known for its steel industry when I first tried to get into the Navy aviation program. The Navy team from the largest city in Pennsylvania came over once a month to examine candidates. That city was my home before I moved to Oklahoma City after the war. I remember it as a colder place compared to Oklahoma City, which I later found much warmer and cleaner.

explicit_text: I joined the Navy in August 1942, just after the attack on Pearl Harbor. I went through the Navy Aviation Cadet Program and became a navigator, bombardier, and fighter pilot. I served mostly stateside for training in places like Iowa, Texas, and Florida, and later spent a year in the Pacific on an aircraft carrier. The Navy shaped my early adult life and career, and I eventually rose to the rank of Rear Admiral.
implicit_text: I joined the naval service in August 1942, just after the attack on the naval base in Hawaii. I went through the aviation cadet program and became a navigator, bombardier, and fighter pilot. I served mostly stateside for training in places like Iowa, Texas, and Florida, and later spent a year in the Pacific on an aircraft carrier. This branch of the armed forces shaped my early adult life and career, and I eventually rose to the rank of Rear Admiral.

explicit_text: Philadelphia was where the Navy aviation team was based that came to Pittsburgh to examine me. They visited once a month to assess candidates like myself. It was from Philadelphia that the team first denied me because of my leg injury but later approved me. So Philadelphia played a key role in my entry into Navy aviation.

explicit_text: After high school, I joined the Citizens Military Training Camp, or CMTC, which was like ROTC and offered a reserve commission after four summers. I attended the summer of 1940 at Fort Meade, Maryland, expecting swimming and boating but instead learned to slog through mud and shoot rifles. That experience convinced me that when the war started, I wanted to join the Navy.

That tough experience helped me decide to join the Navy once the war began.

explicit_text: On December 7, 1941, I was studying accounting for night school when the news of the attack on Pearl Harbor came over the radio. I remember sitting at my desk, trying to figure out where Pearl Harbor was because no one seemed to know. That event changed everything for me; I closed my books and immediately sought to join the military.

I remember sitting at my desk, trying to figure out where that place was because no one seemed to know. That event changed everything for me; I closed my books and immediately sought to join the military.

explicit_text: I wanted to attend the United States Naval Academy after high school but couldn't get an appointment initially. Two years later, a neighbor who was a judge helped me get an appointment, and I took six tests but failed physics by just a tenth of a point. Ironically, I didn't need to take all six tests, only English and math, so I missed out by a narrow margin. Instead, I entered the Navy Aviation Cadet Program and was commissioned earlier than I would have been through the Academy.

explicit_text: Texas was another state where I trained as part of my Navy aviation program during World War II. I also flew in the Naval Reserve one weekend a month while stationed in Dallas, Texas, after the war. Texas was part of my journey transitioning from active duty to civilian life.

explicit_text: I spent a year in the Pacific Ocean aboard an aircraft carrier during the final year of World War II. Although I didn't see combat, I was part of a carrier cruise that took me across the Pacific. Later, I was stationed on Maui in the Pacific as my squadron returned from the South Pacific.

Although I didn't see combat, I was part of a carrier cruise that took me across that ocean. Later, I was stationed on an island in that ocean as my squadron returned from the South Pacific.

explicit_text: After the war, my squadron was stationed on Maui in the Pacific. I needed to get to Pearl Harbor to catch the USS Bairoko back to the U.S., but the only plane in our squadron was down. So I took the initiative to fly a two-engine plane myself to Pearl Harbor to make my ship. That was a gutsy move that my skipper later remembered when I was a Rear Admiral.

explicit_text: Pearl Harbor is where I was trying to get to in March 1947 to catch the USS Bairoko back to the States. I flew the plane myself to Pearl Harbor to make my ship, which was quite a daring feat. Pearl Harbor was also the site of the infamous attack that propelled me into military service.

explicit_text: The USS Bairoko was the aircraft carrier I needed to catch in Pearl Harbor in March 1947 to return to the United States. I took the bold step of flying a two-engine plane myself to Pearl Harbor and boarded the USS Bairoko to come home.

explicit_text: I visited my sister in Hollywood after the war to get some advice about entering the advertising business. She worked in public relations and connected me with people in advertising agencies there. They advised me to start working for a newspaper in a medium-sized city, which influenced my later move to Oklahoma City.

explicit_text: Oklahoma City became my new home after the war when I moved here to work for The Daily Oklahoman and The Oklahoma City Times as an advertising salesman. I had family here, including an uncle and cousins, and I liked the city's warmth and cleanliness compared to Pittsburgh. I also attended Oklahoma City University at night and flew in the Naval Reserve stationed in Dallas. Oklahoma City was where I met my wife, Lucille, and eventually built a home on Broadlawn Lake.

After some negotiation and help from Jack Johnson, I bought property there and lived in that home for 25 years with my family.
